design_philosophy.core_loop: Traverse themed levels, defeat bosses to collect movie memorabilia, unlock final confrontation with Pinky and the Brain
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Time pressure combined with health management across three characters with distinct abilities; resource scarcity of health items
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Control the Warner Siblings as they navigate a film studio lot, using each character's unique powers to solve puzzles and overcome obstacles while collecting iconic props
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Character-specific ability gating (Yakko's paddleball for stunning, Wakko's mallet for switches/fuses, Dot's kiss for NPC triggers) creates mandatory switching rather than optional variety. Timed levels enforce pacing discipline. Optional memorabilia collection creates completion challenge without blocking progression.

difficulty.lives_system: Multiple lives tracked per character. Lives increase via collecting 100 stars or golden character face pickups. Health degrades visually (character faces transition from smiling to tired/unhappy/weak) before death.
difficulty.checkpointing: Level-based checkpoint system; Game Boy version removes entire levels on Easy mode, suggesting difficulty scaling through content removal rather than parameter tuning
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Game Boy version offers three difficulty modes (Easy/Normal/Hard) that alter level selection and progression path. Genesis version implies standard/hard modes affect challenge intensity. Player can attempt levels in any order (Genesis version) suggesting optional difficulty sequencing.

progression.upgrade_system: Temporary power-ups via slot machine (triggered after collecting threshold of coins): invincibility, character revival, coin manipulation. Persistent character unlocks tied to boss defeats.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Boss defeats in any four initial levels unlock fifth level. Completing all five levels unlocks final boss stage. SNES version unlocks Editing Room (boss rush) after completing all other stages. Game Boy Easy mode terminates early; Normal/Hard modes unlock final confrontation after three levels.
replayability_hooks: SNES: Three different endings based on script page collection count (incentivizes completionist replay)
Genesis: Optional non-linear level selection order creates routing variety
Game Boy: Three difficulty modes with different level availability
Memorabilia/script collection provides hidden content hunt across levels

world_building_techniques: Film genre parody level theming (Space Opera, Action Movie, etc.) tied to stage design
Licensed IP integration: Warner Bros. studio setting grounds action in recognizable IP universe
Character-driven motivation: Collecting props for culture shop (Genesis) or recovering stolen script (SNES) frames gameplay objectives as narrative quests
NPC interactions: Dot's kiss ability triggers NPC-specific story progression moments
Final boss narrative: Pinky and the Brain as antagonists provides series-consistent antagonism

boss_design.philosophy: Each level culminates in boss encounter requiring use of specific character abilities or environmental manipulation. Bosses tied to level themes and genre parodies. Final level features boss rush (SNES) combining all previous bosses before ultimate confrontation.
notable_bosses: Pinky and the Brain (final boss, level-appropriate to climax narrative conflict)
Genre-themed bosses corresponding to Science Fiction/Space Opera, Action Movie, and other film parody stages (specific names not documented)

faction_indicators: Warner Siblings vs. Pinky and the Brain (good vs. evil world domination attempt)
Warner Bros. CEO as authority figure requesting assistance
Security guard Ralph as enforcer/antagonist in SNES version
danger_signals: White blockhead robots with red bodies working for Pinky and the Brain indicate organized threat presence
Time pressure on levels creates urgency/danger atmosphere
Character capture/defeat mechanics (SNES) signal escalating peril
environmental_storytelling: Film studio lot setting provides thematic variety through level design (Space Opera, Action Movie, etc.)
Movie memorabilia as collectibles reinforce entertainment industry setting
Script pages scattered in hard-to-reach areas suggest world exploration rewards curiosity
Stage transitions between film genres visually narrate progression through studio productions
